nameMeanings.name: Cochran
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Scottish (Lanarkshire and Renfrewshire): habitational name from the lands of Cochrane in the parish of Paisley, near Glasgow. The placename is of uncertain derivation, perhaps from Welsh coch ‘red’, although this etymology is not supported by the early spelling Coueran.

variants.name: Cothran
meanings: Probably Scottish: variant of   Cochran  .
Perhaps also English: variant of   Cawthorne  .

variants.name: Coughran
meanings: Scottish: variant of   Cochran  . This surname is now rare in Britain.

variants.name: Cohran
meanings: Scottish: variant of   Cochran  .

variants.name: Cohron
meanings: Scottish: variant of   Cochran  .

variants.name: Cothren
meanings: Probably Scottish: variant of   Cochran  .
Perhpas alsoa variant of English   Cawthorne  .

variants.name: Cochren
meanings: Scottish: variant of   Cochran  . This name is extinct in Britain.

variants.name: Cochrane
meanings: Scottish (central Scotland): variant of   Cochran  .

variants.name: Cochrum
meanings: Probably an altered form of Scottish   Cochran   or English   Cockerham  .

variants.name: Caughron
meanings: Altered form of Scottish   Cochran  .
Shortened and altered form of Irish   McCaghren  .

variants.name: Cothron
meanings: Probably Scottish: variant of   Cochran  .
Perhaps also English: variant of   Cawthorne  .

nameMeanings.name: Of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Korean Kyong, Kyong Suk, Young, Chae, Chi Young, Hang, Kil, Rin, Sang Hun, Chang, Chong, Insoo, Kum, Sun Young, Yom, Young Hwan.  Cambodian Kiwan, Oeurn, Phoeun, Pich, Saroeuth, Seung, Sophath, Sophea, Yoeun, Phan, Son.  Indian Shanti, Harendra.
Indian (Uttar Pradesh): Brahmin name, from Sanskrit om, the Hindu sacred and mystic syllable uttered at the beginning of prayers.
Cambodian: written ឱម, corresponding to the exclamation of solemn affirmation, of Sanskrit origin (see 1 above), chanted by Buddhist monks.

variants.name: Oum
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Southeast Asian Heang, Hye, Kam, Kan, Kheng, Khin, Kun, Sang, Sok, Phong, Yom, Yoo, Youn, Cuong, Phal, Samnang, Tin.  Cambodian Sokha, Chheang, Choeun, Moeun, Roeun, Sambath, Samoeun, Savuth, Soeun, Sopheap.
Cambodian: written អ៊ុំ, from a Khmer word denoting an elder sibling of one's parent (i.e. ‘uncle’ or ‘aunt’). Compare   Om   3 and   Um   2.

nameMeanings.name: That
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Probably an Americanized form of German Thiet(e), a variant of Thiede .
English (mainly Huntingdonshire): probably a variant of   Tate  .

variants.name: Thiede
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Gerhard, Erwin, Frieda, Helmut, Herta, Klaus, Kurt, Ralf.
North German: from a pet form of the personal name   Dietrich  .

variants.name: Teate
meanings: Probably an Americanized form of German Thiet (see Teat ) or Thiede .
English: variant of   Teat  . This form of the surname is rare in Britain and Ireland.

nameMeanings.name: Ilk
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Polish Mariusz, Stanislaw, Tadeusz, Zygmunt, Andrzej, Bogdan, Boguslaw, Casimir, Krzysztof, Stanislawa, Boleslaw, Darek.

Polish: from the vocabulary word wilk ‘wolf’, probably applied as a nickname for someone thought to resemble a wolf or connected with wolves in some way. Alternatively, from an Old Slavic personal name based on vlъkъ ‘wolf’.
English (Staffordshire): from Middle English Wilke, a pet form of William and probably a shortening of Wilkin . The usage was very likely Flemish in origin.

variants.name: Wilczak
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Polish Andrzej, Danuta, Stanislawa, Witold, Wojciech.
Polish: patronymic from the personal name   Wilk  .

variants.name: Wilkes
meanings: English (West Midlands): either a variant of Wilk , with genitival or post-medieval excrescent -s, or from the Middle English personal name Wilcus, an uncommon pet form of a personal name in Wil-. The suffix is a double diminutive -k + -us, the latter being used with Old English female names (compare Aldous ). So Wilcus may also be a pet form of an Old English female name such as Wilburg. Alternatively, it may have been used with Norman personal names of either gender, for example as a pet form of William . If Wilcus became an inherited surname it would quickly have become merged with Wilkes. Wilcus may also have been absorbed into Wildgoose .
Dutch and North German: patronymic from the personal name Wilke, a pet form of Will.

variants.name: Wilkosz
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Polish Zbigniew, Henryk.
Polish: derivative of   Wilk  .

variants.name: William
meanings: English (of Norman origin): from the Middle English personal name William, Willam, Willem, an Old Picard and Norman French form of ancient Germanic Willihelm, composed of the elements wil- ‘will, desire’ + helm- ‘helmet, protection’. This Norman personal name became widely used in England, Scotland and Wales after the Conquest, mainly no doubt in honor of the Conqueror himself. By the 13th century it had already become the most popular personal name in England. A Parisian form of the name Guillaume was also sometimes used (see Gillam ), which in medieval Wales was adopted as Gwilym (see Gwilliam ).
Americanized form (translation into English) of French   Guillaume  .

variants.name: Wilczek
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Polish Stanislaw, Casimir, Ewa, Krzystof, Miroslaw, Zosia.  German Gunther, Natascha.
Polish: from a diminutive of the personal name Wilk , from wilk ‘wolf’.

nameMeanings.name: Robert
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the many French names of Germanic origin that were introduced into Britain by the Normans; it has since remained in continuous use. It is derived from the nearly synonymous elements hrōd ‘fame’ + berht ‘bright, famous’, and had a native Old English predecessor of similar form (Hreodbeorht), which was supplanted by the Norman name. Two dukes of Normandy in the 11th century bore the name: the father of William the Conqueror (sometimes identified with the legendary Robert the Devil), and his eldest son. It was borne also by three kings of Scotland, notably Robert the Bruce ( 1274–1329 ), who freed Scotland from English domination. The altered short form Bob is very common, but Hob and Dob, which were common in the Middle Ages and gave rise to surnames, are extinct. See also Rupert .

en.lifeSummary: Robert Cochran Of That Ilk was born before 20 April 1458, in Paisley, Renfrewshire, Scotland as the son of Allan Cochran Of That Ilk and Mrs Allan Cochran. He had at least 1 son with Elizabeth Sempill. He died on 21 April 1504.

fr.lifeSummary: Robert Cochran Of That Ilk est né avant 20 avril 1458, à Paisley, Renfrewshire, Écosse, fils de Allan Cochran Of That Ilk et Mrs Allan Cochran. Il a eu au moins 1 fils avec Elizabeth Sempill. Il est décédé le 21 avril 1504.

de.lifeSummary: Robert Cochran Of That Ilk wurde vor 20. April 1458 in Paisley, Renfrewshire, Scotland, als Sohn von Allan Cochran Of That Ilk und Mrs Allan Cochran geboren. Er hatte mit Elizabeth Sempill mindestens 1 Sohn. Er starb am 21. April 1504.

it.lifeSummary: Robert Cochran Of That Ilk è nato il prima del 20 aprile 1458 nel Paisley, Renfrewshire, Scotland come figlio di Allan Cochran Of That Ilk e Mrs Allan Cochran. Lui avuto almeno 1 figlio con Elizabeth Sempill. Morì il 21 aprile 1504.
